one_line_takeaway: Hallucinations can be detected training-free by measuring the differential entropy (log-det of covariance eigenvalues) of an LLM's own internal sentence embeddings across multiple sampled answers, outperforming logit- and text-level self-consistency baselines.
key_finding: EigenScore consistently outperforms uncertainty- and consistency-based baselines by a large margin on CoQA, SQuAD, and NQ across LLaMA-7B/13B and OPT-6.7B. Concretely (paper's own phrasing), it beats Lexical Similarity by up to 5.6% AUROC on CoQA and 8.9% on SQuAD; e.g. on LLaMA-7B/CoQA EigenScore reaches AUROC(sim) 80.4 vs Lexical Similarity 74.8. On the simple-answer TriviaQA dataset all methods are close and Perplexity is actually best, which the authors attribute to one/two-word answers. Feature clipping consistently helps, with the largest single gain being 1.8% AUROC. Against stronger baselines on CoQA, EigenScore beats SelfCheckGPT, Semantic Entropy, and SentSAR, while being ~10x more efficient than SelfCheckGPT (which needs a separate model). On TruthfulQA, EigenScore (76.7/79.5/81.3 at 5/20/50 prompts) trails the training-based ITI (83.3), which the authors note trains 1024 classifiers on the validation set.
core_contribution: The paper's OWN contribution is INSIDE, a framework for detecting knowledge hallucinations in LLMs directly from their internal states (sentence embeddings) rather than from output logits or decoded text. It has two novel components: (1) EigenScore, a self-consistency metric defined as the log-determinant (1/K · sum of log eigenvalues) of the covariance matrix of K sampled responses' last-token middle-layer embeddings, which the authors prove (Remark 1) equals the differential entropy in the sentence-embedding space; and (2) a test-time feature clipping technique that truncates extreme activations in the penultimate layer (top/bottom 0.2 percentile via a memory bank) to suppress overconfident, self-consistent hallucinations. NOTE: Perplexity, Energy, LN-Entropy, and Lexical Similarity are cited baselines, not this paper's; SelfCheckGPT, Semantic Entropy, SentSAR, and ITI (which reaches 83.3 on TruthfulQA, beating EigenScore) are cited comparison methods, not contributions of this work.
threat_model: Non-adversarial reliability/safety failure mode: no external attacker is assumed. The concern is a deployed LLM emitting confidently wrong or nonfactual ("hallucinated") answers that a downstream user trusts. INSIDE is a detector/selective-generation defense against these unintentional failures, in the lineage of OOD detection (it benchmarks against an Energy-score OOD baseline and adapts activation-rectification ideas from ReAct/ASH). It specifically targets self-consistent (overconfident) hallucinations that consistency-only methods miss.
limitations: The method requires K multiple generations per query (K=10-20 recommended), adding sampling overhead versus single-pass detectors, though the authors show it is ~10x cheaper than model-based SelfCheckGPT and clipping/EigenScore compute is negligible (0.06s). It underperforms on TriviaQA where answers are one or two words (little embedding diversity to exploit). It is beaten on TruthfulQA by the training-based ITI (83.3 vs up to 81.3), though the authors argue ITI's edge comes from training on the validation set and does not generalize. Experiments are confined to QA; applicability to summarization/translation is only conjectured, not demonstrated. Feature clipping's per-neuron percentile thresholds are hand-set (p=0.2 via the three-sigma rule) and the appendix notes activation distributions vary substantially across tokens, making a single clipping threshold risky.

datasets_used: Natural Questions / NQ (3610 QA pairs, validation)
quantitative_results: EigenScore beats Lexical Similarity by up to 5.6% AUROC on CoQA and 8.9% on SQuAD (paper's stated max gains)
LLaMA-7B/CoQA: EigenScore AUCs 80.4 / AUCr 80.8 / PCC 50.8 vs Lexical Similarity 74.8 / 77.8 / 43.5
LLaMA-13B/SQuAD: EigenScore AUCs 83.8 vs Lexical Similarity 77.4
CoQA vs strong baselines (LLaMA-13B AUCs): EigenScore 72.8 vs SelfCheckGPT 68.1, SentSAR 71.4, SemanticEnt 65.3
TruthfulQA accuracy: EigenScore 76.7/79.5/81.3 at 5/20/50 prompts vs ITI 83.3 (ITI trains 1024 classifiers)
EigenScore ~10x more efficient than SelfCheckGPT; clipping+EigenScore compute ~0.06s
Optimal K around 15-20 generations; hidden dim d=4096 (LLaMA-7B), 5120 (LLaMA-13B)
